edges.description: DNMT3A dysfunction perturbs developmental programs required for normal neurodevelopment.
Abnormal developmental regulation contributes to microcephaly and craniosynostosis.
DNMT3A developmental epigenetic dysregulation is associated with the opposing-growth HESJAS phenotype that includes growth failure.
DNMT3A gain-of-function mutations drive accelerated DNA methylation accumulation at lineage-specific genes, mimicking age-related epigenetic alterations.
Neurodevelopmental disruption produces profound global developmental delay.
Impaired cranial growth leads to microcephaly.
Abnormal cranial morphogenesis can lead to premature cranial suture fusion.
Abnormal craniofacial growth and morphogenesis produce facial dysmorphic features.
DNA hypermethylation at lineage-specific genes impairs stem cell maintenance and differentiation capacity across multiple tissue lineages.

Reduced stem cell output and lineage skewing compromise tissue regenerative capacity, contributing to the growth delay phenotype. The potential role of stem cell dysfunction in age-related hematological, bone, and metabolic complications remains to be characterized clinically in HESJAS.

nodes.description: Dysmorphic facial features are part of the recognizable syndrome gestalt.

evidence.reference_title: A novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A associated with craniosynostosis: a case report of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome
evidence.snippet: Physical exam showed microcephaly and facial dysmorphic features

nodes.description: Craniosynostosis has been reported as an additional cranial manifestation of HESJAS.

evidence.reference_title: A novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A associated with craniosynostosis: a case report of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome
A novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A associated with craniosynostosis: a case report of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome
evidence.snippet: Here, we present a case of HESJAS caused by a novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A.
DNMT3A | HGNC:2978 | Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome | MONDO:0032882 | AD | Limited

nodes.description: Pathogenic heterozygous DNMT3A variation disrupts epigenetic regulation during development and establishes the primary molecular lesion in Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome. DNMT3A gain-of-function mutations cause accelerated gains in DNA methylation that recapitulate age-related epigenetic alterations, leading to multilineage stem cell dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: A novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A associated with craniosynostosis: a case report of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome
evidence.snippet: Here, we present a case of HESJAS caused by a novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A.

We demonstrate that DNMT3A gain-of-function mutations in Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome recapitulate age-related gains in DNA methylation (DNAme), cause multilineage stem cell dysfunction, and phenocopy aspects of aging in humans and mice.

nodes.description: DNMT3A gain-of-function mutations cause region-specific accumulation of DNA methylation (hypermethylation) at lineage-specific genes. This epigenetic dysregulation recapitulates age-related patterns of aberrant DNA methylation and directly impairs stem cell function across multiple tissue lineages, reducing stem cell output and causing lineage skewing.

evidence.snippet: We also show that region-specific DNA hypermethylation at lineage-specific genes can explain reduced stem cell output and lineage skewing.

nodes.description: Severe developmental delay is a major manifestation of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: A novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A associated with craniosynostosis: a case report of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome
evidence.snippet: A five-year-old girl presented with severe developmental delay.

nodes.description: Poor postnatal growth is part of the opposing growth phenotype associated with HESJAS.

evidence.reference_title: A novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A associated with craniosynostosis: a case report of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome
evidence.snippet: there are recent reports of variants in the same gene giving rise to an opposing clinical phenotype presenting with microcephaly, growth failure, and impaired development—named Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome (HESJAS).

nodes.description: Altered developmental regulation contributes to reduced head growth and, in some patients, premature cranial suture fusion.

evidence.reference_title: A novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A associated with craniosynostosis: a case report of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome
evidence.snippet: Physical exam showed microcephaly and facial dysmorphic features, and neurodevelopmental assessments revealed profound global developmental delay. Brain magnetic resonance imaging findings were normal; however, brain 3D computed tomography revealed craniosynostosis.

nodes.description: Disrupted developmental regulation downstream of DNMT3A dysfunction drives the severe developmental phenotype of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: A novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A associated with craniosynostosis: a case report of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome
evidence.snippet: A five-year-old girl presented with severe developmental delay.

nodes.description: Reduced head growth is a core clinical feature of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: A novel pathogenic variant of DNMT3A associated with craniosynostosis: a case report of Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome
evidence.snippet: Physical exam showed microcephaly and facial dysmorphic features

nodes.description: Region-specific DNA hypermethylation driven by DNMT3A gain-of-function causes dysfunction of stem/progenitor cells across multiple lineages. This manifests as reduced stem cell output and abnormal lineage skewing, compromising tissue regenerative capacity. In model systems, this mechanism is implicated in age-related hematological, bone, and metabolic dysfunction, though the extent to which these age-related phenotypes manifest clinically in pediatric HESJAS patients remains incompletely characterized.

evidence.snippet: DNMT3A gain-of-function mutations in Heyn-Sproul-Jackson syndrome recapitulate age-related gains in DNA methylation (DNAme), cause multilineage stem cell dysfunction, and phenocopy aspects of aging in humans and mice.
